speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: The person on the left here is my aunt, Aunt Heintje who was married to my father's oldest brother, Isaac, Isaac Voorzanger. And next to her is my cousin, Beppie, who is my-- who was the youngest daughter of my uncle and-- and aunt. And next to her is me. And this picture was taken in 1935 in Ostend in Belgium. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is myself. And I was about, oh, 14 years old, maybe, 14, 15 years old. And this picture was taken in Westmalle in 1944. And also, this picture was taken by the priest who educated me in Catholicism. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in Capelle in 1945, after the war. This is with the De Cock family. And on the left side, on the top row, starting with [? Yido. ?] Then comes Mr. De Cock. Next to him is [? Lucharde ?] Next to him is [? Cordeliva ?] Next to her is me. And I'm coming back from the left side. On the bottom row is Jeff. Next to him is [? Theo ?]. Next to him is Mrs. De Cock, with her daughter [? Josefa ?] on her lap. And next to her is my brother, Joe, with [? Omer ?] in his lap. This taken-- this picture was taken by a professional photographer. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This was the house in Westmalle. I took this picture in 1953 or '54 just to show and keep it as a memory. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My room was way on top of the building in the attic. Was a huge house, as you can see here. He rented it. It was not his own house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But that's where I spent most of my time during the war years. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken when I was doing my service in the Air Force. It was near Delft in-- in Holland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1951, this picture was taken. I was in the photo department. And at this particular time, we were being punished for refusing an order, and as you can see, that we were not very impressed. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is me, Jack Voorzanger, with my then girlfriend, who I married later on. Her name is Ellie, Elisabeth [? Conk. ?] This was-- picture was taken by an amateur. And I hand-tinted it. It was taken in 1953 in the Hague.

speaker: interviewer
text: Country?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Holland. The Netherlands. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is my cousin Rika Voorzanger, who was married to Bernard De Haaf. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She survived the Holocaust by fleeing to southern France and hiding out there for the rest of the war. After the war, she came back and emigrated to the United States. And she and her husband sponsored me to come to this country. And the little boy she is holding there is my son, Freddy Voorzanger. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me, [SIGHS] Jack Voorzanger, with my son, Fred Voorzanger. This picture was taken 1978. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fred just had bought a new camera. And my wife, Juliet, took this picture. After this picture was taken, he left for Hawaii, and we never saw him again. This picture was taken in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on Gibson Street in-- I can't-- what is it? Beverlywood area. And that's in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's in Los Angeles. Yes, Beverlywood, Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Fred Voorzanger, in the Air Force. This picture was taken in 1977, or '78 rather, 1978. It was taken at the Air Force base where he was stationed in Cheyenne. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was a corporal at the time. And he had his picture taken for me and Juliet for our wedding anniversary. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a Honorable Service [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I received from the Air Force. And it says on this, 'honorable service in the Armed Forces of the United States of America, in grateful memory of Sergeant [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Fred Martin Voorzanger, [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] who died while in the service of our country as a member of the United States Air Force on the fourth day of August, 1979. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This certificate is awarded as a testimonial of honest and faithful service.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Signed by the Secretary of the Air Force. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Voorzanger, would you introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wife, Juliet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We will be married-- we will be married 29 years March 25. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And behind her is my daughter-- my oldest daughter, Karin. She lives, unfortunately, far away. She lives in San Francisco with her husband, Jerry. Next to Karen is my second daughter, Olivia, with her baby little boy, Shua, who is four months old. And next to her, last but not least, is my daughter, Vanessa, who lives in Laguna Beach. And here on my lap is my granddaughter, Ashley, who lives with her mommy in San Francisco. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I wanted to ask if anyone in the family wanted to say anything about your father's experiences, about your husband's experience, or what it was like growing up in a survivor home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to say a few words. And I would like to-- to say that I love you very much, and I'm so proud of what you did here today. We know it wasn't easy. And it's just-- two things. You were recognized, once and for all, for what you've been through. And the second thing is that you paid homage to all the ones who perished, And that's the least what we can do. And that's-- that's why we wanted our children to be brought up in this country and made it possible for them to follow their Jewish education and to take pride in their heritage. And in a small way, it's-- it's fulfilling the wishes of parents and grandparents and all that perished. So we love you. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Growing up, I always really admired my dad for his strength and his love. And maybe it's because of the experience that he had during the war that he's always been so giving to all of us. It was never a question. It was never a no. He loved us with all of his heart and always wanted the best for all of us and for all of us to grow up with everything and with a family. And it was always the strength of the family and the support of the family. We were always together and always there for each other. And-- and I love my dad more than anything, and I'm so proud of him for who he is and what he did today. I love you, Daddy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say that I'm very, very proud that he's my father. And growing up and hearing his stories, I just couldn't believe, as a little girl, that my father had been through that. And he's just a really special person, and I'm very proud to call him my dad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was raised in a different house than my father. I didn't hear-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Many of the stories, I didn't hear, but I know what he went through, and it was very difficult for him. [CRYING] And even after the war, his life was very, very difficult. And I also love him very much. I always have. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're very grateful that he survived and I'm glad he has a-- a great wife. I have two great sisters and a new nephew.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a little [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I have this little one, who-- it's always nice to, you know, you have the little ones, who help you-- I don't want to say forget the past, because you don't want to forget the past, but they bring so much-- so much sunshine into your lives. And I know that [LAUGHTER] the grandchildren, for my dad, I know it's-- it's made his life [LAUGHS] easier for him, and a lot of sunshine. And I was-- I'm very glad to have been a part of this and to be able to be here and to share it with everybody. And I want to thank you for doing this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He has a lot to say. [LAUGHTER] [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you all very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
